views.title: Three Values Around an Edge
views.text:
The **arithmetic progression rule** is the single rule that determines all values in the topograph from the home triad.

Consider any edge in the topograph. It is shared by two faces with values **u** and **v**. The edge connects two vertices (nodes in the tree). Around each vertex, three faces meet. The face opposite to one vertex has a value determined by u, v, and the value e at the vertex's third face:

u + v = e + f

where e and f are the values of the faces on opposite sides of the edge's **node**. This is the arithmetic progression (also called the **lax law** or **climbing rule**).

Simpler statement: **across every node, the sum of opposite face values is equal.** Or: the two faces on one side of a node and the two on the other side form an arithmetic progression.
views.activity:
Click on any face in the topograph to inspect it. The sidebar shows the face value, its neighbours, and demonstrates the arithmetic rule f = u + v − e.

Try clicking a face at depth ≥ 2 to see how its value is derived from its two parent values.

quiz.question: Three faces around a node have values 3, 5, and 7. What is the value of the fourth face (opposite the node)?
quiz.explanation: The arithmetic progression rule: u + v = e + f, so f = u + v − e. If u=3, v=5, e=7: f = 3+5−7 = 1.

views.title: Why the Rule Works
views.text:
The arithmetic progression rule is not a coincidence — it follows directly from algebra.

Given two basis vectors e₁ = (p, q) and e₂ = (r, s) with det = ps − qr = ±1, the four vectors at the corners of the 'diamond' are e₁, e₂, e₁+e₂, and −e₁ (or some sign variant).

The bilinear form associated to Q is:
B(u, v) = Q(u + v) − Q(u) − Q(v)

The rule follows from:
Q(e₁ + e₂) + Q(e₁ − e₂) = 2Q(e₁) + 2Q(e₂)

This is the **parallelogram law** for quadratic forms — a consequence of bilinearity.
views.activity:
Verify the rule. For Q = x² + xy + y², click on any face at depth ≥ 2 — the sidebar shows the face value and its parent-edge neighbours, and confirms that f = u + v − e holds at each node.

Try changing the form to Q = x² + 2y² and check whether the rule still applies.

quiz.question: For Q(x,y) = x² + y², you know Q(1,0)=1, Q(1,1)=2, Q(0,1)=1. Using the rule, what is Q(2,1)?

views.title: Step-by-Step Cell Completion
views.text:
You can use the arithmetic progression rule to fill in a topograph cell by cell, starting from the home triad.

Around each **vertex** of the topograph, exactly three faces meet. Label them A, B, C. Then for any edge AB, the two faces at the endpoints of that edge that are NOT A or B satisfy:

A + B = C_left + C_right

where C_left and C_right are the third faces at each endpoint of edge AB.

So if you know three faces of a 'diamond' (rhombus), you can compute the fourth as:
f_new = A + B − f_old
views.activity:
Click on any face in the topograph to inspect it. Try clicking the face at depth 1 adjacent to the home triad for Q = x² + 2y².

The sidebar will show its value and neighbours, and compute f = u + v − e to demonstrate how each value is determined by its parents.

quiz.question: In the topograph, four faces form a 'diamond' around an edge. Three known values are 1, 2, and 3. The fourth (opposite corner) has value:
quiz.explanation: Using f_new = A + B − f_old: with A=1, B=2, f_old=3, the fourth value is 1+2−3=0.

views.title: The Topograph Determines Q Completely
views.text:
A remarkable fact: the topograph completely encodes the quadratic form.

**Theorem**: Two forms Q₁ and Q₂ are equivalent (i.e., one is obtained from the other by an invertible integer change of variables) if and only if their topographs are isomorphic as labeled graphs.

This makes the topograph into a **canonical invariant** of the equivalence class. Conway's insight was that the topograph is a better invariant than the reduced form — it simultaneously shows you the value distribution, the discriminant (via the presence or absence of a river), and the automorphisms (symmetries) of the form.

The **home triad** is just a basepoint — any superbase can serve as a starting point for reading off a, b, c.
views.activity:
Enter the two forms Q₁ = 2x² − xy + 3y² and Q₂ = 3x² + xy + 2y². Switch to the Equivalence Explorer tab. The app draws both topographs side by side.

Are these forms equivalent? Look at whether they share the same value distribution.

quiz.question: If two forms Q₁ and Q₂ have the same topograph structure (same labeled graph up to isomorphism), what can you conclude?
options: They have the same discriminant only
They are equivalent — related by a unimodular change of variables
They represent the same set of primes only
quiz.explanation: The topograph is a complete invariant of the equivalence class: two forms are equivalent if and only if their topographs are isomorphic as edge-labeled trees.
